content.text: Customer Communication Templates for GenAI Incidents

Writing customer-facing communications during an active incident is one of the most difficult writing tasks in engineering. The author has partial information, is under time pressure, and must be simultaneously accurate, reassuring, and legally careful. Pre-authored templates solve this by resolving the writing problem before the incident happens. During the incident, the Comms Lead fills in variables and posts—rather than composing from scratch under stress.
Core Concept

A customer communication template pre-determines structure, tone, what must be included, and what must never be included. Templates do not eliminate judgment—the Comms Lead still decides which template to use and fills in the specifics. Templates eliminate blank-page paralysis and ensure that legal, compliance, and tone requirements are met by default rather than by memory.
GenAI incidents require specialized templates

because the nature of the failure is often different from traditional infrastructure incidents. Output quality degradation, safety policy violations, and model behavior changes require language that describes user experience impact without disclosing internal model architecture or creating legal admissions.
Architecture: Communication Decision Tree

attrs.alt: Customer communication decision tree showing incident type routing to appropriate templates
attrs.title: Customer Communication Decision Tree

content.text: Template A: Investigating (No Root Cause)
Used immediately on SEV-1 declaration, before root cause is known. Goal: acknowledge the issue, describe user-visible impact, and set update expectations without speculating.

Subject: [Service Name] — We Are Investigating an Issue

We are currently investigating an issue affecting {affected_component}.
Users may be experiencing {user_visible_symptom}.

We are actively working to identify and resolve this issue.
We will provide an update by {next_update_time} UTC.

We apologize for the disruption.

--- Fill-in guide ---
affected_component: 'the API', 'our AI assistant', 'response generation'
user_visible_symptom:
  - 'errors when submitting requests'
  - 'increased response times'
  - 'responses that do not match your input'
  - 'failed completions'
next_update_time: current time + 15 min (SEV-1) or + 30 min (SEV-2)
Template B: Identified (Root Cause Known, Fix in Progress)

Used after the root cause is confirmed. Include what was found at a customer-understandable level, what is being done, and an ETA. Do not include internal service names, architecture details, or speculative future causes.

Subject: [Service Name] — Issue Identified, Fix Underway

We have identified the cause of the issue affecting {affected_component}:
{root_cause_customer_description}.

We are currently {mitigation_description}.
Estimated resolution: {eta_description}.
Next update: {next_update_time} UTC, or sooner if status changes.

--- Fill-in guide ---
root_cause_customer_description (use user-visible language, not internal tech):
  - 'a configuration change that affected request routing'
  - 'increased load on our infrastructure exceeding planned capacity'
  - 'an issue with an upstream dependency'
  - 'a recent update that introduced unexpected behavior'

mitigation_description:
  - 'rolling back the configuration change'
  - 'scaling up capacity to handle current traffic'
  - 'routing traffic to a backup system'

eta_description (only if confident, otherwise omit):
  - '30-45 minutes'
  - 'within the next hour'
  - 'under investigation — we will confirm ETA in the next update'
Template C: Resolved
The final customer-facing communication. Must include the resolution time, the affected period, a brief factual description of the impact, and a postmortem commitment.

Subject: [Service Name] — Resolved

This incident has been resolved.

{affected_component} returned to normal operation at {resolution_time} UTC.

Impact summary: Between {start_time} and {resolution_time} UTC,
{user_visible_impact}. {workaround_note}

We are conducting a full review of this incident and will publish a
summary of our findings and preventive actions at:
{postmortem_url} within {postmortem_days} business days.

We apologize for the disruption and appreciate your patience.

--- Fill-in guide ---
user_visible_impact:
  - 'users experienced elevated error rates of approximately X% for API requests'
  - 'response times increased by an average of X seconds'
  - 'approximately X% of requests to {component} failed'

workaround_note (include if users should take action):
  - 'Requests that failed during this period should be retried.'
  - 'No action is required on your part.'

postmortem_url: status.company.com/incidents/{incident_id}
postmortem_days: 5 for SEV-1, 10 for SEV-2
Template D: GenAI Output Quality Degradation

Traditional incident templates describe infrastructure failures in terms users understand—errors, timeouts, unavailability. GenAI incidents often involve output quality degradation that doesn't fit this pattern. Users receive responses that work technically but are wrong, incomplete, or inappropriate. Template D addresses this class of incident.

Subject: [Service Name] — We Are Aware of Response Quality Issues

We are currently investigating reports of {quality_issue_description}.

Some users may be experiencing {user_visible_quality_symptom}.
{scope_note}

We are actively investigating the cause and working to restore
consistent response quality. We will provide an update by {next_update_time} UTC.

In the meantime, {temporary_guidance}.

--- Fill-in guide ---
quality_issue_description (user-facing, no internal model terms):
  - 'responses that do not fully address the submitted request'
  - 'responses that appear incomplete or truncated'
  - 'inconsistent response quality'

user_visible_quality_symptom:
  - 'shorter or less detailed responses than expected'
  - 'responses that do not follow the requested format'
  - 'responses that appear unrelated to the input'

scope_note (be specific if possible):
  - 'This appears to affect {X type of request} specifically.'
  - 'This affects a subset of users in {region/tier}.'
  - 'We are unable to confirm the scope while investigation continues.'

temporary_guidance:
  - 'retrying the request may produce a better response'
  - 'simplifying the request may help'
  - 'no workaround is available; we are prioritizing resolution'
Template E: Data Exposure or Privacy Incident

This template requires legal review before sending. Data exposure notifications have specific legal requirements under GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and other frameworks. The following is a starting structure only—legal must adapt it to the specific regulatory requirements and confirmed scope.
Template Implementation and Automation
Store templates in a version-controlled repository. Link them from the war room bot so the Comms Lead can retrieve the correct template with a single command:
What Never Appears in Customer Comms
Templates protect against inclusion of content that should never appear in customer-facing communications:
Internal service names and architecture.
'The Prometheus scraper failed to reach the embedding service shard-3' is for the war room only.
Model names and prompt details.
Never reveal the specific model version, prompt template, or guardrail mechanism in customer communications.
Blame attribution.
Do not name vendors, team members, or third parties as responsible. Use passive voice: 'a configuration change was introduced' rather than '[vendor] deployed a breaking change.'
Speculative root cause.
'We believe the issue may have been caused by' creates expectations and potential legal exposure if the speculation is wrong. Wait for confirmation.
Unrealistic promises.
'This will never happen again' is never appropriate. 'We are implementing additional safeguards to reduce the likelihood of recurrence' is accurate.
Customer Communication Review Process

Even with templates, customer communications require a review step before sending. The review process must be fast—measured in minutes, not hours—while still catching errors that could worsen the situation.
Comms Lead drafts from template.
Fill all template variables. Replace all fill-in guide notes. Review against the 'never include' list.
IC reviews in 2 minutes or less.
IC checks: Is the impact description accurate? Are any technical details that shouldn't be public included? Is the tone appropriate? Does it set realistic expectations?
Legal review for flagged types.
Data exposure, safety incidents, and SLA breaches require explicit legal sign-off. Route to legal on-call immediately—do not send while waiting.
Comms Lead sends.
Post to status page, send email blast if configured, and notify the customer support team about the content so they can use it in active tickets.
Log the send time.
Record in the war room timeline: '[HH:MM UTC] Comms Lead: External update posted—Investigating. Next update scheduled HH:MM.'
Adapting Templates for Different Customer Segments

Not all customers receive the same level of communication. Enterprise customers under SLA contracts, API-only customers, and dashboard-only users have different needs and different levels of technical sophistication.
Segment
Communication Channel
Technical Level
SLA Notification?
Enterprise API customers
Status page + proactive email + TAM call
Higher — include error codes, affected endpoints
Required per contract
Self-serve API customers
Status page + email (if subscribed)
Medium — include HTTP error details
Status page only
End-user consumers
Status page + in-app banner
Low — use plain language only
No contractual SLA
Enterprise Customer Direct Notification

Enterprise customers under SLA contracts may require proactive direct notification beyond the status page—typically via email to their technical contact within 15-30 minutes of a SEV-1. The template includes additional context appropriate for a technical audience:

Subject: [URGENT] {Company Name} API Incident — {incident_id}

Dear {customer_name},

We are contacting you directly because you are subscribed to our Enterprise
Service Agreement.

We are currently experiencing an incident affecting {affected_service}.
Your account may be impacted.

Observable impact: {technical_impact_description}
Affected endpoints: {endpoint_list} (or 'under investigation')
Error characteristics: {error_type} — e.g., '5xx responses', 'latency > Xs'
Started: {start_time} UTC
Current status: Investigating / Mitigating

We will send you direct updates every {interval} minutes until resolution.

Your Technical Account Manager {tam_name} is available at {tam_contact}.
For urgent issues, use the enterprise support line: {support_phone}.

We take our SLA commitments seriously and will provide a full incident
report including SLA impact analysis within {sla_report_days} business days.

{Company Name} Operations Team
Post-Incident Communication Follow-Through
Customer communication does not end at resolution. Three follow-through actions maintain customer trust after the incident closes:
Postmortem publication.

Publish a customer-facing postmortem summary within 5 business days for SEV-1. The summary describes what happened, the impact, what was done, and what is being done to prevent recurrence. It does not include internal blame, speculation about other failures, or internal system detail.
SLA credit notification.

If the incident triggered a contractual SLA breach, proactively notify affected enterprise customers of the credit they are owed before they request it. Waiting for customers to claim credits they are owed creates distrust.
Support ticket resolution.

Close all support tickets opened during the incident with a link to the postmortem and resolution summary. Customers who opened tickets deserve a direct resolution confirmation, not just a status page update.
Key Takeaways
Pre-author all templates before incidents happen. The right time to write a customer communication template is during a quarterly incident review drill, not during a live SEV-1.

GenAI incidents need output-quality templates. Standard infrastructure templates describe errors and timeouts; they don't address response quality degradation, which is the most common failure mode in GenAI systems.
Data exposure templates require legal review. Never send a data exposure or privacy notification without legal sign-off, regardless of time pressure.

Templates constrain what must never be included. The value of a template is not just structure—it's the negative space: the fields and language patterns that force writers to stay within appropriate boundaries.
